role_summary: SNCC executive secretary 1961-1966; architect of much of SNCC's national organizational structure; later founder of the International Black Workers Congress.

photo.license: Public domain (U.S., published 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

ai_reading: Six corpus voices discuss Forman: Judy Richardson (entry 69, SNCC documentation), Freddie Greene Biddle (entry 44), Walter Tillow (entry 124, the +1.00 Strategic Vision pole), Gwendolyn M. Patton (entry 52), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34, SNCC Washington office director under Forman), and Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53). Several of these are figures whose own pages identify Forman directly as a manager or director-figure in their movement work, the cosine pattern confirming the organizational hierarchy Forman built.
biographical_paragraph: James Forman is discussed in this corpus by 6 interviewees across the catalog (Judy Richardson (entry 69), Freddie Greene (Biddle) (entry 44), Walter Tillow (entry 124), Gwendolyn M. Patton (entry 52), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34), Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (born Gwen Robinson) (entry 53)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats James Forman as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Walter Tillow
interview_snippets.lead_in: Walter Tillow recounts how the Chicago Friends of SNCC operation hired the staff member who would run the national organization:
interview_snippets.quote: And that guy, if they hired, was James [Jim] Forman. And became executive director of SNCC.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons describes the phone call in which Forman, as SNCC's executive secretary, named her director of the Laurel, Mississippi project:
interview_snippets.quote: It happened very quickly. And so when James Forman calls me and says, you've got to step up. I'm trying to find somebody to send. But we don't have anybody right now. Everybody's tied up. So we need you to become the project director.

interview_snippets.speaker: Judy Richardson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Judy Richardson, who worked in the SNCC Atlanta national office, describes how Forman delegated to younger staff:
interview_snippets.quote: So what's interesting to me is that Forman had a way of giving you as much responsibility as he thought you could take and more than you thought you could take.

interview_snippets.speaker: Courtland Cox
interview_snippets.lead_in: Courtland Cox credits Forman with articulating SNCC's rationale for organizing in white communities:
interview_snippets.quote: I mean, I think the basic physician of SNCC and Jim Forman articulated best, he said, look, the problem that we face is not in the black community. The problems we face are in the white community about the way they feel about things.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn M. Patton
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn M. Patton recalls Forman as an early supporter of the Tuskegee student organizers in her TIAL group:
interview_snippets.quote: But James Forman who is an organizer and a tactician, marveled at it. And so he really became the mentor for tidal kids on campus.

sources.title: James Forman, The Making of Black Revolutionaries
sources.publisher: Macmillan, 1972 (reprinted University of Washington Press, 1997); WorldCat OCLC 376094
